era_context: The Second Triumvirate (Octavian, Antony, Lepidus) is consolidating power following the civil wars of the 40s BCE. The proscriptions and land confiscations continue. In the East, the Parthian Empire is expanding under Orodes II. In Egypt, Cleopatra VII is establishing herself as a major political player. This is a period of transition from the chaos of the late Republic to the eventual stability of the Principate.

events.title: Perusia (Perugia) besieged by Octavian; Lucius Antonius defeated
events.description: Lucius Antonius, brother of Mark Antony, led a revolt against Octavian's land confiscations in Italy. Octavian besieged Lucius and his forces at Perusia (modern Perugia) in a brutal campaign lasting several months. The siege ended with Lucius's surrender and the execution of many of his supporters. This conflict, sometimes called the Perusine War, demonstrated Octavian's ruthlessness and his determination to consolidate control over Italy. It also revealed tensions within the triumvirate, as Mark Antony was nominally allied with Octavian but his brother was in open rebellion.
key_figures: Lucius Antonius (brother of Mark Antony, rebel leader)
Mark Antony (triumvir, nominally allied with Octavian)
Fulvia (wife of Antony, supporter of Lucius)
events.certainty_note: The Perusine War is well-attested in multiple ancient sources. The date 41 BCE is secure from the chronology of the triumviral period.
cross_references: -40-001 (Treaty of Tarentum, which preceded this conflict)

events.title: Fulvia dies; Antony's wife and political ally removed
events.description: Fulvia, the wife of Mark Antony and a powerful political figure in her own right, died in 41 BCE, likely in Sicyon. Fulvia had been a major player in the Perusine War and had actively opposed Octavian's policies. Her death removed a significant obstacle to reconciliation between Antony and Octavian and contributed to the negotiation of the Treaty of Tarentum in 40 BCE. Her death also marked the end of an era in which women of the Roman elite wielded direct political power.
events.certainty_note: The date of Fulvia's death is given as 40 BCE in some sources and 41 BCE in others. The uncertainty is ±1 year. Most modern scholars place it in 40 BCE, but the exact date is not definitively established.
cross_references: -41-001 (Perusine War, in which Fulvia was a key player)

events.title: Antony campaigns in Parthia; initial preparations
events.description: Mark Antony began preparations for a major campaign against the Parthian Empire, aiming to recover Roman prestige after the Parthian invasion of Syria and Judaea in 40 BCE. Antony gathered forces and made diplomatic overtures to client kings in the East. The campaign would not be launched until 36 BCE, but the planning and preparation began in this period. This represented Antony's attempt to match Octavian's consolidation of power in the West with a major military victory in the East.
key_figures: Orodes II (Parthian king, target of campaign)
events.certainty_note: The timing of Antony's preparations for the Parthian campaign is somewhat uncertain. Sources indicate he was planning it in the early 40s BCE, but the exact year is not always clear.

events.title: Cleopatra VII consolidates power in Egypt
events.description: Cleopatra VII continued to consolidate her power in Egypt during this period, navigating the complexities of Roman civil war and Parthian expansion. She maintained her position as a client of Rome while building her own political and economic base. Cleopatra was already in contact with Mark Antony and would become his key ally in the East. Her control of Egypt's vast resources would prove crucial to Antony's ambitions.
events.certainty_note: Cleopatra's consolidation of power was a gradual process. The year 41 BCE is not marked by a specific event but rather represents a period of her continued rule and influence.

events.title: Mark Antony and Cleopatra VII meet; alliance begins
events.description: Mark Antony and Cleopatra VII met at Tarsus in Cilicia, beginning a political and personal alliance that would reshape the Eastern Mediterranean. According to later sources, Antony was immediately captivated by Cleopatra's intelligence and charm. This meeting was ostensibly about coordinating against the Parthians, but it quickly became a personal relationship as well. Cleopatra provided crucial financial and military support for Antony's ambitions, while Antony offered her Roman military backing and legitimacy.
events.certainty_note: The meeting at Tarsus is traditionally dated to 41 BCE, but some sources place it in 40 BCE. The exact date is uncertain by ±1 year. The meeting is well-attested, but the romantic elements are likely embellished in later sources.
cross_references: -40-001 (Treaty of Tarentum, which preceded Antony's Eastern campaigns)

disconfirming_evidence: The date of Fulvia's death is disputed; some sources place it in 40 BCE rather than 41 BCE. The meeting between Antony and Cleopatra at Tarsus is traditionally dated to 41 BCE, but some modern scholars argue for 40 BCE. The romantic narrative surrounding their meeting, as presented in Plutarch and later sources, is likely embellished; the relationship was fundamentally a political and military alliance. The extent of Cleopatra's 'consolidation' of power in 41 BCE is not marked by specific events in the sources; it is a gradual process that is difficult to date precisely.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 41 BCE is rich but relies heavily on later compilations by Appian, Plutarch, and Dio Cassius. The Perusine War is well-documented, but the sources reflect Octavian's perspective as the victor. The narrative of Antony and Cleopatra's meeting is heavily romanticized in later sources, particularly Plutarch, who wrote nearly 200 years after the events. Contemporary coins and inscriptions provide some corroboration for major events. The sources are heavily focused on Rome and the Mediterranean; events in the Parthian Empire, India, and China are not covered due to source limitations in the Western tradition.

graph_edges.note: The Perusine War and its aftermath contributed to the need for reconciliation between Octavian and Antony, leading to the Treaty of Tarentum.
Fulvia's death removed an obstacle to reconciliation between Octavian and Antony.
The alliance between Antony and Cleopatra was foundational to Antony's Parthian campaign.
